addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

"""
========================================
Interpolation (:mod:`scipy.interpolate`)
========================================

.. currentmodule:: scipy.interpolate

Sub-package for functions and objects used in interpolation.

See the :ref:`user guide <tutorial-interpolate>` for recommendations on choosing a
routine, and other usage details.


Univariate interpolation
========================

.. autosummary::
   :toctree: generated/

   make_interp_spline
   CubicSpline
   PchipInterpolator
   Akima1DInterpolator
   FloaterHormannInterpolator
   BarycentricInterpolator
   KroghInterpolator
   CubicHermiteSpline

**Low-level data structures for univariate interpolation:**

.. autosummary::
   :toctree: generated/

   PPoly
   BPoly
   BSpline


Multivariate interpolation
==========================

**Unstructured data**

.. autosummary::
   :toctree: generated/

   LinearNDInterpolator
   NearestNDInterpolator
   CloughTocher2DInterpolator
   RBFInterpolator

**For data on a grid:**

.. autosummary::
   :toctree: generated/

   RegularGridInterpolator

.. seealso::

    `scipy.ndimage.map_coordinates`,
    :ref:`An example wrapper for map_coordinates <tutorial-interpolate_cartesian-grids>`


**Low-level data structures for tensor product polynomials and splines:**


.. autosummary::
   :toctree: generated/

   NdPPoly
   NdBSpline


1-D spline smoothing and approximation
======================================

.. autosummary::
   :toctree: generated/

   make_lsq_spline
   make_smoothing_spline
   make_splrep
   make_splprep
   generate_knots

Rational Approximation
======================

.. autosummary::
   :toctree: generated/

   AAA


Interfaces to FITPACK routines for 1D and 2D spline fitting
===========================================================

This section lists wrappers for `FITPACK <http://www.netlib.org/dierckx/>`__
functionality for 1D and 2D smoothing splines. In most cases, users are better off
using higher-level routines listed in previous sections.


1D FITPACK splines
------------------

This package provides two sets of functionally equivalent wrappers: object-oriented and
functional.

**Functional FITPACK interface:**


.. autosummary::
   :toctree: generated/

   splrep
   splprep
   splev

**Object-oriented FITPACK interface:**

.. autosummary::

2D FITPACK splines
------------------

**For data on a grid:**

.. autosummary::

   RectBivariateSpline
   RectSphereBivariateSpline

**For unstructured data (OOP interface):**

.. autosummary::

   SmoothSphereBivariateSpline
   LSQBivariateSpline
   LSQSphereBivariateSpline

**For unstructured data (functional interface):**

.. autosummary::

Additional tools
================

   lagrange

   pade

   barycentric_interpolate

   Rbf
   interp1d
   interp2d

.. seealso::

   `scipy.ndimage.map_coordinates`,
   `scipy.ndimage.spline_filter`,

import warnings
import os

from numpy import zeros, asarray, eye, poly1d, hstack, r_
from scipy import linalg
from scipy._lib._array_api import xp_capabilities

__all__ = ["pade"]

@xp_capabilities(out_of_scope=True)
def pade(an, m, n=None):
    """
    Return Pade approximation to a polynomial as the ratio of two polynomials.

    .. deprecated:: 1.18.0
        This function is deprecated and will be removed in SciPy 1.20.0. Use
        `mpmath.pade` instead.

    Parameters
    ----------
    an : (N,) array_like
        Taylor series coefficients.
    m : int
        The order of the returned approximating polynomial `q`.
    n : int, optional
        The order of the returned approximating polynomial `p`. By default,
        the order is ``len(an)-1-m``.

    Returns
    -------
    p, q : Polynomial class
        The Pade approximation of the polynomial defined by `an` is
        ``p(x)/q(x)``.

    Examples
    --------
    >>> import numpy as np
    >>> from scipy.interpolate import pade
    >>> e_exp = [1.0, 1.0, 1.0/2.0, 1.0/6.0, 1.0/24.0, 1.0/120.0]
    >>> p, q = pade(e_exp, 2)

    >>> e_exp.reverse()
    >>> e_poly = np.poly1d(e_exp)

    Compare ``e_poly(x)`` and the Pade approximation ``p(x)/q(x)``

    >>> e_poly(1)
    2.7166666666666668

    >>> p(1)/q(1)
    2.7179487179487181

    """
    _warn_skips = (os.path.dirname(__file__),)
    msg = ("`pade` is deprecated and will be removed in SciPy 1.20.0. Use "
           "`mpmath.pade` instead.")
    warnings.warn(msg, DeprecationWarning, skip_file_prefixes=_warn_skips)
    an = asarray(an)
    if n is None:
        n = len(an) - 1 - m
        if n < 0:
            raise ValueError("Order of q <m> must be smaller than len(an)-1.")
    if n < 0:
        raise ValueError("Order of p <n> must be greater than 0.")
    N = m + n
    if N > len(an)-1:
        raise ValueError("Order of q+p <m+n> must be smaller than len(an).")
    an = an[:N+1]
    Akj = eye(N+1, n+1, dtype=an.dtype)
    Bkj = zeros((N+1, m), dtype=an.dtype)
    for row in range(1, m+1):
        Bkj[row,:row] = -(an[:row])[::-1]
    for row in range(m+1, N+1):
        Bkj[row,:] = -(an[row-m:row])[::-1]
    C = hstack((Akj, Bkj))
    pq = linalg.solve(C, an)
    p = pq[:n+1]
    q = r_[1.0, pq[n+1:]]
    return poly1d(p[::-1]), poly1d(q[::-1])

file.content:
"""
Here we perform some symbolic computations required for the N-D
interpolation routines in `interpnd.pyx`.

"""
from sympy import symbols, binomial, Matrix


def _estimate_gradients_2d_global():

    #
    # Compute
    #
    #

    f1, f2, df1, df2, x = symbols(['f1', 'f2', 'df1', 'df2', 'x'])
    c = [f1, (df1 + 3*f1)/3, (df2 + 3*f2)/3, f2]

    w = 0
    for k in range(4):
        w += binomial(3, k) * c[k] * x**k*(1-x)**(3-k)

    wpp = w.diff(x, 2).expand()
    intwpp2 = (wpp**2).integrate((x, 0, 1)).expand()

    A = Matrix([[intwpp2.coeff(df1**2), intwpp2.coeff(df1*df2)/2],
                [intwpp2.coeff(df1*df2)/2, intwpp2.coeff(df2**2)]])

    B = Matrix([[intwpp2.coeff(df1).subs(df2, 0)],
                [intwpp2.coeff(df2).subs(df1, 0)]]) / 2

    print("A")
    print(A)
    print("B")
    print(B)
    print("solution")
    print(A.inv() * B)

file.content:
# Impl routines common for all backends
from itertools import combinations_with_replacement
from math import comb

def _monomial_powers_impl(ndim, degree):
    """Return the powers for each monomial in a polynomial.

    Parameters
    ----------
    ndim : int
        Number of variables in the polynomial.
    degree : int
        Degree of the polynomial.

    Returns
    -------
    (nmonos, ndim) int ndarray
        Array where each row contains the powers for each variable in a
        monomial.

    """
    nmonos = comb(degree + ndim, ndim)
    out = [[0]*ndim for _ in range(nmonos)]
    count = 0
    for deg in range(degree + 1):
        for mono in combinations_with_replacement(range(ndim), deg):
            # `mono` is a tuple of variables in the current monomial with
            # multiplicity indicating power (e.g., (0, 1, 1) represents x*y**2)
            for var in mono:
                out[count][var] += 1
            count += 1
    return out

30	template<typename T>

33	fprota(T c, T s, T a, T b)
34	{
35	    return std::make_tuple(

55	        mesg = mesg + std::to_string(size) + " in dimension = " + std::to_string(dim);

66	struct Array1D

68	    T* data;

"""
Routines for evaluating and manipulating piecewise polynomials in
local power basis.

"""

import numpy as np

cimport cython

cimport libc.stdlib
cimport libc.math

from scipy.linalg.cython_lapack cimport blas_int, dgeev

include "_poly_common.pxi"

DEF MAX_DIMS = 64

#------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Piecewise power basis polynomials
#------------------------------------------------------------------------------

@cython.wraparound(False)
@cython.boundscheck(False)
@cython.cdivision(True)
def evaluate(const double_or_complex[:,:,::1] c,
             const double[::1] x,
             const double[::1] xp,
             int dx,
             bint extrapolate,
             double_or_complex[:,::1] out):
    """
    Evaluate a piecewise polynomial.

    Parameters
    ----------
    c : ndarray, shape (k, m, n)
        Coefficients local polynomials of order `k-1` in `m` intervals.
        There are `n` polynomials in each interval.
        Coefficient of highest order-term comes first.
    x : ndarray, shape (m+1,)
        Breakpoints of polynomials.
    xp : ndarray, shape (r,)
        Points to evaluate the piecewise polynomial at.
    dx : int
        Order of derivative to evaluate.  The derivative is evaluated
        piecewise and may have discontinuities.
    extrapolate : bint

"""
Cythonized routines for the RegularGridInterpolator.
"""

from libc.math cimport NAN
import numpy as np
cimport numpy as np
cimport cython

include "_poly_common.pxi"

np.import_array()


@cython.wraparound(False)
@cython.boundscheck(False)
@cython.initializedcheck(False)
def evaluate_linear_2d(const double_or_complex[:, :] values, # cannot declare as ::1
                       const np.intp_t[:, :] indices,        # unless prior
                       const double[:, :] norm_distances,    # np.ascontiguousarray
                       tuple grid not None,
                       double_or_complex[:] out):
    cdef:
        long num_points = indices.shape[1]      # XXX: npy_intp?
        long i0, i1, point
        double_or_complex y0, y1, result

    if grid[0].shape[0] == grid[1].shape[0] == 1:
        # single grid point along both axes: no interpolation
        for point in range(num_points):
            out[point] = values[0, 0]
    elif grid[1].shape[0] == 1:
        # single grid line along axis=1: linear interpolation along axis=0 alone
        for point in range(num_points):
            i0 = indices[0, point]
            if i0 >= 0:
                y0 = norm_distances[0, point]
                result = values[i0, 0]*(1 - y0) + values[i0+1, 0]*y0
                out[point] = result
    elif grid[0].shape[0] == 1:
        # single grid line along axis=0: linear interpolation along axis=1 alone
        for point in range(num_points):
            i1 = indices[1, point]
            if i1 >= 0:
                y1 = norm_distances[1, point]
                result = values[0, i1]*(1 - y1) + values[0, i1+1]*y1
                out[point] = result
    else:
        # linear interpolation along both axes
        for point in range(num_points):
            i0, i1 = indices[0, point], indices[1, point]
            if i0 >=0 and i1 >=0:
                y0, y1 = norm_distances[0, point], norm_distances[1, point]

                result = 0.0
                result = result + values[i0, i1] * (1 - y0) * (1 - y1)
                result = result + values[i0, i1+1] * (1 - y0) * y1
                result = result + values[i0+1, i1] * y0 * (1 - y1)
                result = result + values[i0+1, i1+1] * y0 * y1
                out[point] = result

    return np.asarray(out)


@cython.wraparound(False)
@cython.boundscheck(False)
@cython.cdivision(True)
@cython.initializedcheck(False)
def find_indices(tuple grid not None, const double[:, :] xi):
    # const is required for xi above in case xi is read-only
    cdef:
        long i, j, grid_i_size
        double denom, value
        # const is required in case grid is read-only
        const double[::1] grid_i

        # Axes to iterate over
        long I = xi.shape[0]
        long J = xi.shape[1]

        int index = 0

        # Indices of relevant edges between which xi are situated
        np.intp_t[:,::1] indices = np.empty_like(xi, dtype=np.intp)

        # Distances to lower edge in unity units
        double[:,::1] norm_distances = np.zeros_like(xi, dtype=float)

    # iterate through dimensions
    for i in range(I):
        grid_i = grid[i]
        grid_i_size = grid_i.shape[0]

        if grid_i_size == 1:
            # special case length-one dimensions
            for j in range(J):
                # Should equal 0. Setting it to -1 is a hack: evaluate_linear 
                # looks at indices [i, i+1] which both end up =0 with wraparound. 
                # Conclusion: change -1 to 0 here together with refactoring
                # evaluate_linear, which will also need to special-case
                # length-one axes
                indices[i, j] = -1
                # norm_distances[i, j] is already zero
        else:
            for j in range(J):
                value = xi[i, j]
                index = find_interval_ascending(&grid_i[0],
                                                grid_i_size,
                                                value,
                                                prev_interval=index,
                                                extrapolate=1)
                indices[i, j] = index

                if value == value:
                    denom = grid_i[index + 1] - grid_i[index]
                    norm_distances[i, j] = (value - grid_i[index]) / denom
                else:
                    # xi[i, j] is nan
                    norm_distances[i, j] = NAN


    return np.asarray(indices), np.asarray(norm_distances)
=====POLY_COMMON=====
# common bits and pieces for _ppoly.pyx and _rgi_cython.pyx

ctypedef double complex double_complex

ctypedef fused double_or_complex:
    double
    double complex


@cython.wraparound(False)
@cython.boundscheck(False)
@cython.cdivision(True)
cdef int find_interval_ascending(const double *x,
                                 size_t nx,
                                 double xval,
                                 int prev_interval=0,
                                 bint extrapolate=1) noexcept nogil:
    """
    Find an interval such that x[interval] <= xval < x[interval+1]. Assuming
    that x is sorted in the ascending order.
    If xval < x[0], then interval = 0, if xval > x[-1] then interval = n - 2.

    Parameters
    ----------
    x : array of double, shape (m,)
        Piecewise polynomial breakpoints sorted in ascending order.
    xval : double
        Point to find.
    prev_interval : int, optional
        Interval where a previous point was found.
    extrapolate : bint, optional
        Whether to return the last of the first interval if the
        point is out-of-bounds.

    Returns
    -------
    interval : int
        Suitable interval or -1 if nan.

    """
    cdef:
        int high, low, mid
        int interval = prev_interval
        double a = x[0]
        double b = x[nx - 1]
    if interval < 0 or interval >= nx:
        interval = 0

    if not (a <= xval <= b):
        # Out-of-bounds (or nan)
        if xval < a and extrapolate:
            # below
            interval = 0
        elif xval > b and extrapolate:
            # above
            interval = nx - 2
        else:
            # nan or no extrapolation
            interval = -1
    elif xval == b:
        # Make the interval closed from the right
        interval = nx - 2
    else:
        # Find the interval the coordinate is in
        # (binary search with locality)
        if xval >= x[interval]:
            low = interval
            high = nx - 2
        else:
            low = 0
            high = interval

        if xval < x[low+1]:
            high = low

        while low < high:
            mid = (high + low)//2
            if xval < x[mid]:
                # mid < high
                high = mid
            elif xval >= x[mid + 1]:
                low = mid + 1
            else:
                # x[mid] <= xval < x[mid+1]
                low = mid
                break

        interval = low

    return interval

# FITPACK Module Map for scipy.interpolate

## 1. _fitpack_py.py: Functional FITPACK Interface (Lines 1-907)

**Location:** `/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/scipy-scipy/scipy/interpolate/_fitpack_py.py`

This module provides the **public functional API** for FITPACK. All functions are marked as `legacy` with recommendations for newer alternatives.

| Function | Lines | Description |
|----------|-------|-------------|
| `splprep` | 14-161 | Find B-spline representation of an N-D parametric curve. Takes list of arrays x, returns (tck, u) tuple. Uses parcur from FITPACK. |
| `splrep` | 164-306 | Find B-spline representation of 1-D curve y=f(x). Returns (t, c, k) tuple (knots, coefficients, degree). Uses curfit from FITPACK. |
| `splev` | 309-397 | Evaluate B-spline or its derivatives at points x. Takes tck tuple and der parameter. Delegates to _fitpack_impl or BSpline.__call__. |
| `splint` | 400-468 | Evaluate definite integral of B-spline between a and b. Can use BSpline.integrate() or delegate to _fitpack_impl. |
| `sproot` | 471-564 | Find roots of cubic B-spline only (k=3). Returns array of zeros. For non-cubic, recommends PPoly.from_spline().roots(). |
| `spalde` | 567-663 | Evaluate B-spline and ALL derivatives up to order k at given point(s). Returns array/list of derivatives. Does NOT accept BSpline instances. |
| `insert` | 666-765 | Insert knots into B-spline. Returns new tck tuple or BSpline. Wraps FITPACK insert routine. |
| `splder` | 768-833 | Compute spline representation of derivative (order n). Returns BSpline or tuple. Inverse of splantider. |
| `splantider` | 836-905 | Compute spline for antiderivative (integral, order n). Returns BSpline or tuple. Uses cumulative sum formula. |
| `bisplrep` | (imported from impl) | Find bivariate B-spline representation of surface. Returns [tx, ty, c, kx, ky]. |
| `bisplev` | (imported from impl) | Evaluate bivariate B-spline and partial derivatives. |

## 2. _fitpack_impl.py: Low-level C Extension Wrapper (Lines 1-829)

**Location:** `/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/scipy-scipy/scipy/interpolate/_fitpack_impl.py`

**Role:** This module wraps the **C extension `_fitpack`** (which wraps Dierckx FITPACK Fortran). It handles data validation, workspace allocation, and state caching for FITPACK's iterative algorithms.

### Key Components:

| Component | Lines | Description |
|-----------|-------|-------------|
| `_iermess`, `_iermess2` | 49-97 | Error message dictionaries mapping FITPACK error codes to messages/exceptions |
| `_parcur_cache` | 99-101 | Cache for parametric curve fitting state (between task=0 and task=1 calls) |
| `_curfit_cache` | 211-212 | Cache for univariate curve fitting state |
| `_surfit_cache` | 413-414 | Cache for bivariate surface fitting state |

### Key Functions (implementations of _fitpack_py API):

| Function | Lines | Description |
|-----------|-------|-------------|
| `splprep` | 104-208 | Parametric curve fitting. Calls `_fitpack.parcur`. Manages _parcur_cache. |
| `splrep` | 215-301 | Univariate spline fitting. Calls `_fitpack.curfit` or `_fitpack.percur` for periodic. |

## 4. _fitpack_repro.py: Reproducible Spline Functions (Lines 1-1341)

**Location:** `/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/scipy-scipy/scipy/interpolate/_fitpack_repro.py`

**Purpose:** Provides **deterministic, reproducible** alternatives to legacy FITPACK. Addresses the problem that FITPACK's knot selection and smoothing algorithms can behave differently across platforms or due to implementation details.

### Problem Solved vs Old FITPACK:
- Old FITPACK uses iterative algorithms with caches and mutable state
- Knot placement can vary due to floating-point differences
- `make_splrep`/`make_splprep` use pure-Python implementations of the same algorithms for reproducibility

### Key Functions:

| Function | Lines | Description |
|-----------|-------|-------------|
| `generate_knots` | 164-274 | **Generator** that yields successive knot vectors of increasing length until LSQ criterion is satisfied. Implements FITPACK's knot placement algorithm in pure Python. |
| `_generate_knots_impl` | 277-408 | Core implementation. Uses TOL=0.001, MAXIT=20. Adds knots in intervals with maximum residuals via add_knot(). |
| `make_splrep` | 1014-1164 | Create smoothing B-spline function. Returns BSpline object. Uses generate_knots + root-finding for smoothing parameter p. |
| `make_splprep` | 1167-1340 | Parametric version of make_splrep. Returns (BSpline, u) tuple. For curves in N-D space. |

### Supporting Classes/Functions:

| Component | Lines | Description |
|-----------|-------|-------------|
| `F` | 515-611 | Represents f(p) = residual - s for non-periodic case. Uses QR decomposition and discontinuity matrix. |
| `Fperiodic` | 613-779 | Periodic version of F. Uses augmented matrices G1, G2, H1, H2. |
| `disc` | 442-512 | Discontinuity matrix: jumps of k-th derivatives at internal knots. |
| `root_rati` | 828-918 | Solve f(p)=0 using rational function approximation. Replicates FITPACK's fprati.f algorithm. |
| `fprati` | 782-798 | Compute root of r(p) = (u*p+v)/(p+w) from three bracket points. |
| `add_knot` | 74-95 | Add new knot at interval with maximum residuals. Calls _dierckx.fpknot. |

### Algorithm Overview:
1. **Knot Selection**: Uses `generate_knots` which mimics FITPACK's fpcurf.f algorithm
2. **Smoothing**: Uses parameter p (1/p is weight on smoothness vs data fit)
3. **Root-finding**: Rational interpolation (FITPACK's fprati) to find p such that fp(p) = s
4. **Stopping**: When abs(fp - s) < s*TOL (TOL = 0.001) or max iterations (20) reached

## 5. Overall Architecture & Relationships

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

### What is FITPACK (Dierckx)?

**FITPACK** is a Fortran library by **Paul Dierckx** for curve and surface fitting with splines. Key characteristics:

- **Algorithms**: Smoothing splines that balance data fidelity (sum of squared residuals) and smoothness (sum of k-th derivative jumps at knots)
- **Knot Selection**: Automatic knot placement based on residuals
- **Smoothing Parameter**: Controls trade-off via parameter s (target sum of squared residuals) or p (weight on smoothness)
- **Routines**: curfit (1-D), parcur (parametric), surfit (bivariate scattered), regrid (bivariate gridded), sphere (spherical)

**Two C Extensions:**
1. **_fitpack**: Original netlib FITPACK (f2c-generated C)
2. **_dierckx**: C++ implementation with additional routines (fpknot, qr_reduce, fpback, etc.) used by _fitpack_repro.py

└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

                              │  │ make_splprep     │ │
                              │  │ generate_knots   │ │

                              │  │  FITPACK calls)   │ │
                              │  └──────────────────┘ │
                              └──────────────────────┘
```

### What is FITPACK (Dierckx)?

**FITPACK** is a Fortran library by **Paul Dierckx** for curve and surface fitting with splines. Key characteristics:

- **Algorithms**: Smoothing splines that balance data fidelity (sum of squared residuals) and smoothness (sum of k-th derivative jumps at knots)
- **Knot Selection**: Automatic knot placement based on residuals
- **Smoothing Parameter**: Controls trade-off via parameter s (target sum of squared residuals) or p (weight on smoothness)
- **Routines**: curfit (1-D), parcur (parametric), surfit (bivariate scattered), regrid (bivariate gridded), sphere (spherical)

**Two C Extensions:**
1. **_fitpack**: Original netlib FITPACK (f2c-generated C)
2. **_dierckx**: C++ implementation with additional routines (fpknot, qr_reduce, fpback, etc.) used by _fitpack_repro.py

# Core B-Spline Module Analysis: `_bsplines.py`

## 1. PUBLIC Classes and Functions (from `__all__` at lines 22-23)

### Main Exports:
- **BSpline** (lines 469-1202) - Univariate spline in B-spline basis; stores (t, c, k, extrapolate) and provides evaluation, derivatives, antiderivatives, integration, knot insertion, and design matrix construction
- **make_interp_spline** (lines 1642-1922) - Factory function for creating interpolating B-splines with specified degree and boundary conditions (not-a-knot, natural, clamped, periodic)
- **make_lsq_spline** (lines 1926-2135) - Creates smoothing B-splines using least squares criterion with optional weights
- **make_smoothing_spline** (lines 2481-2697) - Creates cubic smoothing spline using Generalized Cross Validation (GCV) to find optimal regularization parameter

## 2. Key Internal Helper Functions

### Knot Construction (lines 1278-1433):
- **_not_a_knot** (lines 1278-1297) - Constructs knot vector with not-a-knot boundary conditions following de Boor XIII(12)
- **_augknt** (lines 1300-1302) - Constructs knot vector by adding k copies of endpoints
- **_periodic_knots** (lines 1415-1432) - Returns knots on a circle for periodic splines

### Interpolation Support (lines 1435-1633):
- **_make_interp_per_full_matr** (lines 1435-1495) - Full matrix solver for periodic interpolation when n <= k
- **_handle_lhs_derivatives** (lines 1498-1538) - Fills colocation matrix entries for known derivatives at boundaries
- **_make_periodic_spline** (lines 1541-1633) - Computes coefficients for periodic B-spline using Woodbury formula optimization
- **_woodbury_algorithm** (lines 1329-1412) - Solves cyclic banded systems with upper-right/lower-left blocks

### Boundary Condition Processing (lines 1305-1327):
- **_convert_string_aliases** (lines 1305-1313) - Converts "clamped"/"natural" string aliases to derivative specifications
- **_process_deriv_spec** (lines 1316-1327) - Processes derivative specifications into orders and values

### LSQ Support (lines 2138-2185):
- **_lsq_solve_qr** (lines 2162-2184) - QR factorization solver for LSQ spline coefficients
- **_lsq_solve_qr_for_root_rati_periodic** (lines 2142-2159) - Periodic variant for root-finding

### Smoothing Spline Support (lines 2188-2477):
- **_compute_optimal_gcv_parameter** (lines 2193-2444) - Computes optimal lambda via Generalized Cross Validation
- **_coeff_of_divided_diff** (lines 2447-2477) - Returns coefficients of divided differences for penalty matrix

### Knot Insertion (lines 1204-1271):
- **_insert** (lines 1204-1271) - Inserts a single knot; port of Dierckx FORTRAN routine

### Utility (lines 26-72):
- **_get_dtype** (lines 26-31) - Returns complex128 for complex dtypes, float64 otherwise
- **_as_float_array** (lines 34-44) - Converts input to C-contiguous float array
- **_dual_poly** (lines 47-55) - Dual polynomial of B-spline basis element
- **_diff_dual_poly** (lines 58-71) - d-th derivative of dual polynomial

### Backend Support (lines 409-433):
- **_get_xp_bspline_cls** (lines 409-433) - Returns backend-specific BSpline class (NumPy, CuPy, etc.)

### FITPACK Look-alike (lines 2700-2846):
- **fpcheck** (lines 2704-2846) - Validates consistency of data points x and knot vector t; clone of FITPACK fpchec/fpchep

## 3. BSpline Data Structure and Evaluation

### Internal `_BSpline` class (lines 74-407):
- **Attributes stored**: `t` (knots), `c` (coefficients), `k` (degree), `extrapolate` (bool/'periodic'), `axis`
- **__init__** (lines 82-125): Validates inputs, normalizes axis, converts to correct dtypes
- **construct_fast** (lines 127-141): Creates spline without validation checks
- **basis_element** (lines 143-156): Returns single B-spline basis element
- **design_matrix** (lines 158-217): Returns sparse CSR design matrix of basis evaluations
- **__call__** (lines 219-264): Evaluates spline via `_dierckx.evaluate_spline` (C/Fortran backend)
- **integrate** (lines 277-358): Computes definite integral via antiderivative or FITPACK splint
- **from_power_basis** (lines 360-391): Converts from power basis (e.g., CubicSpline) to B-spline
- **insert_knot** (lines 393-406): Inserts new knot at given position

### Public `BSpline` class (lines 469-1202):
- **Delegates to `_BSpline`** for NumPy backend; supports other backends via array namespace
- **Properties** (lines 663-715): `t`, `c`, `k`, `extrapolate`, `axis`, `tck` (read-only tuple)
- **basis_element** (lines 717-780): Classmethod for creating basis element
- **design_matrix** (lines 782-862): Classmethod for sparse design matrix
- **__call__** (lines 864-890): Evaluates spline with optional derivative order `nu`
- **derivative** (lines 892-929): Returns derivative BSpline via `_fitpack_impl.splder`
- **antiderivative** (lines 931-980): Returns antiderivative via `_fitpack_impl.splantider`
- **integrate** (lines 982-1032): Definite integral computation
- **from_power_basis** (lines 1034-1116): Converts piecewise polynomial to B-spline
- **insert_knot** (lines 1118-1191): Inserts knot(s) at position

## 4. How `make_interp_spline` Computes Spline

### Knot Construction (lines 1836-1844):
- If `t=None` and no derivatives: uses `_not_a_knot` or `_periodic_knots`
- If derivatives specified: uses `_augknt` (repeats endpoints k times)

### BC Type Handling (lines 1781-1800):
- String aliases: "not-a-knot", "natural", "clamped", "periodic"
- Periodic checks: validates y[0] == y[-1]

### Matrix Setup (lines 1860-1908):
- **Colocation matrix** (lines 1891-1898): Built via `_dierckx._coloc` in banded storage
- **Boundary conditions** (lines 1894-1898): Fills derivative rows via `_handle_lhs_derivatives`
- **RHS** (lines 1900-1907): Combines y-values with derivative values at boundaries

### Banded Solve (lines 1909-1920):
- Uses LAPACK `gbsv` (general banded solver) via `get_lapack_funcs`
- Solves `Ab @ x = rhs` where Ab is banded matrix

### Special Cases (lines 1808-1827):
- **k=0**: Piecewise constant, t = [x, x[-1]]
- **k=1**: Piecewise linear, t = [x[0], x, x[-1]]

## 5. PPoly/BPoly Relationship

**Note**: PPoly, BPoly, and NdPPoly are NOT in this file - they are in `_interpolate.py`.

The connection between B-splines and piecewise polynomials:
- **BSpline.from_power_basis** (lines 1034-1116): Converts CubicSpline (power basis) to B-spline basis using Marsden's identity
- **_fitpack_impl.splder/splantider** (lines 928, 973): Used in derivative/antiderivative, work with tck representation

## 6. Notable Algorithms and Mechanisms

### B-spline Basis Evaluation:
- **Recursive definition** documented in docstring (lines 530-534)
- **Efficient evaluation** via `_dierckx.evaluate_spline` (line 253) - C/Fortran backend
- **Design matrix** via `_dierckx.data_matrix` (line 201)

### Knot Insertion:
- **Boehm's algorithm** implemented in `_insert` (lines 1204-1271)
- References Boehm (1980) and Dierckx (1993)
- Handles periodic case with boundary adjustments

### Periodic Splines:
- **Cyclic banded solver** via `_woodbury_algorithm` (lines 1329-1412)
- **Woodbury formula** for efficient solving of systems with corner blocks
- **Periodic knots** via `_periodic_knots` (lines 1415-1432)

### Least Squares Smoothing:
- **Two methods** (line 1926): "norm-eq" (normal equations) or "qr" (QR factorization)
- **QR reduction** via `_dierckx.qr_reduce` and `_dierckx.fpback` (lines 2171-2173)
- **Weighted LSQ**: `min ||W*(A@c - y)||^2`

### GCV Smoothing Spline:
- **Regularized problem**: `min ||W*(X@c - y)||^2 + lambda*||c^T E c||` (lines 2493-2496)
- **Optimal lambda** via minimization of GCV criterion (lines 2436-2414)
- **Natural spline basis** transformation from B-spline basis (lines 2634-2696)
- References: Wahba (1990), Woltring (1986), Hutchinson & de Hoog (1985)

### Schoenberg-Whitney Conditions:
- Validated in `fpcheck` (lines 2704-2846)
- Ensures: `t(j) < x(j) < t(j+k+1)` for well-posed interpolation

## 7. Relationships to FITPACK and Other Modules

### Direct Dependencies:
- **`_dierckx` module** (line 12): P. Dierckx's C routines for:
  - `evaluate_spline` (line 253)
  - `data_matrix` (line 201)
  - `evaluate_all_bspl` (lines 1473, 1475, 1488, 1532)
  - `find_interval` (line 1222)
  - `_coloc` (lines 1612, 1893)
  - `_norm_eq_lsq` (line 2104)
  - `qr_reduce`, `fpback` (lines 2171-2173)
  - `data_matrix_periodic`, `qr_reduce_periodic`, `fpbacp` (lines 2151-2183)

- **`_fitpack_impl` module** (line 13): FITPACK Python wrappers for:
  - `splint` (line 299): Spline integration
  - `splantider` (lines 307, 973): Spline antiderivative
  - `splder` (line 928): Spline derivative

### FITPACK Compatibility:
- **`fpcheck`** (lines 2704-2846): Clone of FITPACK fpchec/fpchep for validation
- **TCK representation**: `BSpline.tck` property (lines 664-667) returns `(t, c, k)` tuple compatible with FITPACK
- **Note**: No `from_tck` classmethod in this file - conversion from FITPACK would be elsewhere

### Array Backend Support:
- **`array_namespace`** (lines 18-20, 615-634): Supports NumPy, CuPy, and other array libraries
- **`_get_xp_bspline_cls`** (lines 409-433): LRU-cached backend selection

<result>## RBF and Rational Interpolation in scipy.interpolate

### 1. Legacy Rbf Class (_rbf.py)

**Class definition and legacy status:** Lines 56-150, with legacy warning at lines 64-67: "`Rbf` is legacy code, for new usage please use `RBFInterpolator` instead."

**Kernel functions (lines 153-172):** The class implements RBF kernels as methods starting with `_h_`:
- `_h_multiquadric`: `sqrt((r/self.epsilon)**2 + 1)` (line 154)
- `_h_inverse`: `1.0/sqrt((r/self.epsilon)**2 + 1)` (line 157)
- `_h_gaussian`: `exp(-(r/self.epsilon)**2)` (line 160)
- `_h_linear`: `r` (line 163)
- `_h_cubic`: `r**3` (line 166)
- `_h_quintic`: `r**5` (line 169)
- `_h_thin_plate`: `r**2 * log(r)` (line 172)

**Building the interpolant (lines 220-270):** The `__init__` method:
- Stores flattened coordinates in `self.xi` and values in `self.di` (lines 225-238)
- Computes default `epsilon` as average distance between nodes (lines 245-252)
- Computes weights by solving `linalg.solve(self.A, self.di)` (line 270), where `self.A` is the kernel matrix with smoothing subtracted from diagonal

**Kernel matrix (lines 272-277):** Property `A` computes pairwise distances via `squareform(pdist(self.xi.T, self.norm))` and applies the RBF function.

**Evaluation (lines 282-292):** `__call__` computes distances from evaluation points to data points via `cdist`, applies the RBF, and returns `dot(_function(r), nodes)`.

---

### 2. Modern RBFInterpolator (_rbfinterp.py)

**Class definition (lines 69-220):** Full docstring with mathematical formulation.

**Constructor parameters (lines 74-118):**
- `y`: data point coordinates (npoints, ndims)
- `d`: data values (npoints, ...)
- `neighbors`: nearest neighbors for local interpolation
- `smoothing`: smoothing parameter (default 0.0)
- `kernel`: RBF type ('linear', 'thin_plate_spline', 'cubic', 'quintic', 'multiquadric', 'inverse_multiquadric', 'inverse_quadratic', 'gaussian')
- `epsilon`: shape parameter
- `degree`: polynomial degree (lines 106-118 show minimum degrees per kernel)

**Mathematical formulation (lines 121-151):** The interpolant is `f(x) = K(x, y)a + P(x)b` where coefficients solve:
```
(K(x, y) + λI)a + P(y)b = d
P(y)^T a = 0
```

**Constructor implementation (lines 226-349):**
- Lines 232-233: Select backend (_rbfinterp_np or _rbfinterp_xp)
- Lines 316-335: For `neighbors=None`, call `_build_and_solve_system` to get `shift`, `scale`, `coeffs`
- Lines 338-339: For `neighbors!=None`, build KDTree for nearest neighbor queries

**Evaluation (__call__, lines 449-540):**
- Lines 479-486: Without neighbors, use `_chunk_evaluator` with precomputed coefficients
- Lines 487-536: With neighbors, query KDTree (line 493), build unique neighborhoods (lines 502-504), solve local systems (lines 521-529), and evaluate (lines 530-536)
- Lines 375-447: `_chunk_evaluator` handles memory budgeting by chunking large inputs

**Backend dispatch (lines 51-54):** `_get_backend` returns `_rbfinterp_np` for NumPy arrays, `_rbfinterp_xp` otherwise.

---

### 3. Backend Strategy (_rbfinterp_*.py)

**_rbfinterp_common.py (lines 1-33):** Contains `_monomial_powers_impl`, a pure Python function generating monomial exponent combinations for polynomial terms. Shared by all backends.

**_rbfinterp_np.py (NumPy + Pythran backend):**
- Lines 13-89: `_build_and_solve_system` calls LAPACK's `dsysv` via `scipy.linalg.lapack` for symmetric indefinite system solving
- Lines 17-22: Trampolines to pythran-compiled functions
- Lines 91-95: `compute_interpolation` constructs evaluation coefficients and performs `vec @ coeffs`

**_rbfinterp_pythran.py (Pythran-compiled kernels):**
- Lines 4-48: Kernel function definitions and `NAME_TO_FUNC` mapping
- Lines 79-84, 88-92: Pythran-exported `_kernel_matrix` and `_polynomial_matrix`
- Lines 101-164: `_build_system` constructs the LHS/RHS matrices with domain scaling (shift/scale for numerical stability, lines 136-144)
- Lines 174-215: `_build_evaluation_coefficients` constructs coefficients for evaluation

**_rbfinterp_xp.py (Array API backend, lines 1-27):** Module docstring explains the strategy:
> "The general logic is this: `_rbfinterp.py` implements the user API and calls into either `_rbfinterp_np` (the 'numpy backend'), or `_rbfinterp_xp` (the 'generic backend')."
- Uses vectorized code instead of explicit loops for torch/jax compatibility
- Kernels accept array of norms, not scalar
- Kernels accept `xp` argument for array namespace

**Architecture:** Call chain is `_rbfinterp.py` → `_rbfinterp_np.py` → `_rbfinterp_pythran.py` for NumPy, or `_rbfinterp.py` → `_rbfinterp_xp.py` for other array API compliant libraries (PyTorch, JAX, CuPy).

---

### 4. Rational Interpolation (_bary_rational.py)

**Barycentric form (lines 91-133 in base class):** The evaluation uses the barycentric rational form:
```python
r = CC @ (weights * support_values) / (CC @ weights)
```
where `CC = 1 / subtract.outer(z, support_points)` is a Cauchy matrix.

**AAA class (lines 213-630):** Implements the AAA (Adaptive Antoulas-Anderson) algorithm for rational approximation.
- Lines 214-428: Extensive docstring describing the algorithm
- Lines 458-560: `_compute_weights` implements the greedy AAA iteration:
  - Lines 479-490: Select next support point maximizing residual
  - Lines 496-524: Solve least-squares problem via SVD of Loewner matrix
  - Lines 531-539: Compute rational approximant from weights
- Lines 276-282 (docstring): Barycentric form `r(z) = Σ w_j f_j / (z - z_j) / Σ w_j / (z - z_j)`
- Lines 562-629: `clean_up` method removes Froissart doublets (pole-zero pairs with negligible residue)

**AAA support point selection (lines 476-545):** Greedy algorithm selects point maximizing `|f(z) - n(z)/d(z)|` at each iteration.

**FloaterHormannInterpolator (lines 632-753):** 
- Lines 633-730: Docstring explaining C∞ smooth rational interpolant with no poles on real axis
- Lines 669-684 (docstring): Blends `n-d` polynomials of degree `d` with blending functions `λ_i(x)`
- Lines 690-701 (docstring): Barycentric form with weights computed via alternating sums of reciprocal products
- Lines 743-752: `_compute_weights` implements Floater-Hormann weight computation

**Key difference:** AAA is adaptive (greedy support selection) for complex domains, while Floater-Hormann uses all points with precomputed weights for guaranteed real-axis stability.</result>

## FILE 1: `/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/scipy-scipy/scipy/interpolate/_cubic.py`

### 1. PUBLIC CLASSES AND FUNCTIONS

**CubicHermiteSpline** (lines 85-175)
Piecewise cubic Hermite interpolator fitting values and first derivatives with C1 smoothness. Converts data points and derivatives into PPoly coefficient representation via Hermite basis formulas.

**PchipInterpolator** (lines 184-353)
Shape-preserving PCHIP monotonic cubic interpolator (C1 smooth) using weighted harmonic mean of slopes to prevent overshoot. Implements Fritsch-Butland algorithm for derivative computation at interior and boundary points.

**pchip_interpolate** (lines 356-415)
Convenience function wrapper creating PchipInterpolator and evaluating it, optionally returning derivatives.

**Akima1DInterpolator** (lines 426-637)
"Visually pleasing" Akima interpolator (C1 smooth) using local slope weighting to avoid oscillations; supports both classic Akima and modified Makima methods. Extrapolation defaults to False.

**CubicSpline** (lines 647-1065)
Piecewise cubic interpolator with C2 continuity solving tridiagonal system for derivative values at knots; supports not-a-knot, periodic, natural, and clamped boundary conditions.

### 2. KEY INTERNAL HELPERS AND BASE CLASSES

**prepare_input** (lines 21-74)
Validates and prepares input arrays: converts dtypes, checks finiteness/strictly-increasing property, computes dx differences, and moves interpolation axis to position 0.

**PchipInterpolator._edge_case** (lines 281-294)
One-sided three-point estimate for endpoint derivatives with shape-preserving adjustments (zeroing or clamping when sign changes detected).

**PchipInterpolator._find_derivatives** (lines 296-353)
Core PCHIP algorithm computing derivatives via weighted harmonic mean: w1=2hk+h(k-1), w2=hk+2h(k-1), with zero derivatives when slope signs differ.

**CubicSpline._validate_bc** (lines 996-1064)
Validates and normalizes boundary condition specifications, converting 'natural'/'clamped' strings to (order, value) tuples and checking periodic y[0]==y[-1].

### 3. CUBICSPLINE BOUNDARY CONDITIONS IMPLEMENTATION

**not-a-knot** (lines 957-962, 972-977)
Makes first/second segments share same polynomial at each end. Modifies tridiagonal matrix: A[1,0]=dx[1], A[0,1]=x[2]-x[0] with computed RHS based on slopes.

**periodic** (lines 904-955)
Enforces y'[0]==y'[-1] and y''[0]==y''[-1]. Reduces to (n-1) system, uses "condensed matrix" technique solving two tridiagonal systems (s1, s2) for corner values.

**clamped** (lines 1031-1032, 963-966)
First derivative set to zero at boundaries. Matrix: A[1,0]=1, A[0,1]=0, RHS=b[0]=0. For arbitrary value v: RHS=bc_start[1].

**natural** (lines 1033-1034, 967-970)
Second derivative set to zero at boundaries. Matrix: A[1,0]=2*dx[0], A[0,1]=dx[0], RHS based on finite difference approximation of second derivative.

**Special case n=3 with not-a-knot** (lines 859-877)
Fits parabola through three points using 3x3 dense matrix instead of tridiagonal system.

**Special case n=2** (lines 849-853)
Reduces to linear interpolation with derivatives equal to slope.

**periodic** (lines 904-955)
Enforces y'[0]==y'[-1] and y''[0]==y''[-1]. Reduces to (n-1) system, uses "condensed matrix" technique solving two tridiagonal systems (s1, s2) for corner values.

**clamped** (lines 1031-1032, 963-966)
First derivative set to zero at boundaries. Matrix: A[1,0]=1, A[0,1]=0, RHS=b[0]=0. For arbitrary value v: RHS=bc_start[1].

**natural** (lines 1033-1034, 967-970)
Second derivative set to zero at boundaries. Matrix: A[1,0]=2*dx[0], A[0,1]=dx[0], RHS based on finite difference approximation of second derivative.

**Special case n=3 with not-a-knot** (lines 859-877)
Fits parabola through three points using 3x3 dense matrix instead of tridiagonal system.

**Special case n=2** (lines 849-853)
Reduces to linear interpolation with derivatives equal to slope.

### 4. NOTABLE MECHANISMS

**Cubic Hermite Basis** (lines 162-171 in CubicHermiteSpline.__init__)
Coefficients computed as: c = stack([t/dxr, (slope-dydx[:-1])/dxr-t, dydx[:-1], y[:-1]])
where t = (dydx[:-1] + dydx[1:] - 2*slope) / dxr captures curvature.

**Monotone PCHIP** (lines 323-338 in _find_derivatives)
When adjacent slope signs differ or either is zero, derivative set to zero. Otherwise uses weighted harmonic mean: 1/dk = (w1/mk[k-1] + w2/mk[k]) / (w1+w2).

**Akima Weights** (lines 587-615 in Akima1DInterpolator.__init__)
For classic Akima: w1 = |delta[i+1] - delta[i]|, w2 = |delta[i-1] - delta[i-2]|
For Makima: adds |delta[i+1] + delta[i]|/2 term to avoid overshoot and edge cases.
Slope: d[i] = 0.5*(m[i+3] + m[i-3]) initially, then refined via weighted average.

**Tridiagonal Solver** (lines 888-990)
Uses scipy.linalg.solve_banded((1,1), A, b) for efficient O(n) solution of C2 continuity equations.

---

## FILE 2: `/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/scipy-scipy/scipy/interpolate/_polyint.py`

### 1. PUBLIC CLASSES AND FUNCTIONS

**_Interpolator1D** (lines 22-149)
Base class handling common univariate interpolation: axis manipulation, dtype management (float64/complex128), and y-array reshaping.

**_Interpolator1DWithDerivatives** (lines 151-248)
Mixin base adding derivative evaluation capabilities via _evaluate_derivatives method.

**KroghInterpolator** (lines 251-396)
C∞ polynomial interpolator supporting repeated x values for derivative constraints; uses divided differences algorithm for coefficient computation.

**krogh_interpolate** (lines 398-461)
Convenience function creating KroghInterpolator and evaluating it with optional derivatives.

**approximate_taylor_polynomial** (lines 465-556)
[DEPRECATED] Estimates Taylor polynomial via fitting at clustered points using cosine sampling on interval; returns poly1d.

**BarycentricInterpolator** (lines 559-971)
Numerically stable Lagrange interpolator using barycentric form with efficient y-updates and incremental node addition; supports derivative evaluation via recursive interpolation.

**barycentric_interpolate** (lines 974-1051)
Convenience function creating BarycentricInterpolator and evaluating with optional derivatives.

**lagrange** (not found in these files - likely in separate module)

### 2. KEY INTERNAL HELPERS AND BASE CLASSES

**_isscalar** (lines 17-19)
Utility checking if value is scalar or 0-dimensional array.

**_Interpolator1D._prepare_x** (lines 97-101)
Flattens input x to 1D while preserving original shape for reshaping output.

**_Interpolator1D._finish_y** (lines 103-112)
Reshapes interpolated y back to N-D form with correct axis ordering.

**_Interpolator1D._reshape_yi** (lines 114-120)
Moves interpolation axis to position 0 and flattens trailing dimensions.

**_Interpolator1D._set_dtype** (lines 142-148)
Enforces float64 or complex128 dtype based on input dtype.

**KroghInterpolator coefficient computation** (lines 338-355)
Divided differences algorithm handling repeated x values for derivative conditions. Uses factorial scaling for multiplicities.

### 3. BARYCENTRICINTERPOLATOR: BARYCENTRIC WEIGHTS ALGORITHM

**Initial weight computation** (lines 743-767 in __init__)
If weights wi not provided, computed as:
- Capacity scaling: _inv_capacity = 4.0 / (max(xi) - min(xi))
- Random permutation applied to inputs for numerical stability
- For each i: dist[j] = _inv_capacity * (xi[i] - xi[permuted[j]])
- wi[i] = 1.0 / prod(dist) with dist[i] = 1.0 (self-distance handled)

**Explicit formulas** (documented in lines 620-665)
- Equally spaced: wi = (-1)^i * C(n, i) where C is binomial coefficient
- Chebyshev points: wi = (-1)^i for interior, wi = (-1)^i/2 for endpoints

**Incremental node addition** (lines 832-840 in add_xi)
When adding new points, old weights are inverted, updated via:
- wi[:j] *= _inv_capacity * (xi[new] - xi[:j]) for all existing j
- wi[new] = prod(_inv_capacity * (xi[:new] - xi[new])) for new point
- Then re-invert all weights

### 4. NOTABLE MECHANISMS

**Polynomial interpolation via barycentric form** (lines 620-626, 867-884)
Evaluation uses: p(x) = sum(wi * yi / (x - xi)) / sum(wi / (x - xi))
Avoids explicit polynomial coefficients; handles x == xi cases via zero detection.

**Derivative evaluation** (lines 909-971)
Uses recursive relationship: p'(x) computed via interpolating derivative weights c[i,j] = (wj/wi)/(xi[i]-xi[j]) where diagonal c[j,j] = -sum(c[i,j]) for i != j. Creates nested BarycentricInterpolator for c_ij @ yi.

**Krogh divided differences** (lines 338-355)
Handles repeated x values: when xi[k-s] == xi[k], uses factorial scaling Vk[0] = yi[k]/factorial(s). Builds coefficient c via incremental recurrence.

**Axis handling** (lines 103-112, 114-120)
Sophisticated axis manipulation: moves interpolation axis to position 0 for computation, then restores original axis ordering via transpose with index permutation.

- _isscalar imported from _polyint (line 15) for scalar checking
- Both files use scipy.linalg.solve and solve_banded for tridiagonal systems

<result>## RBF and Rational Interpolation in scipy.interpolate

### 1. Legacy Rbf Class (_rbf.py)

**Class definition and legacy status:** Lines 56-150, with legacy warning at lines 64-67: "`Rbf` is legacy code, for new usage please use `RBFInterpolator` instead."

**Kernel functions (lines 153-172):** The class implements RBF kernels as methods starting with `_h_`:
- `_h_multiquadric`: `sqrt((r/self.epsilon)**2 + 1)` (line 154)
- `_h_inverse`: `1.0/sqrt((r/self.epsilon)**2 + 1)` (line 157)
- `_h_gaussian`: `exp(-(r/self.epsilon)**2)` (line 160)
- `_h_linear`: `r` (line 163)
- `_h_cubic`: `r**3` (line 166)
- `_h_quintic`: `r**5` (line 169)
- `_h_thin_plate`: `r**2 * log(r)` (line 172)

**Building the interpolant (lines 220-270):** The `__init__` method:
- Stores flattened coordinates in `self.xi` and values in `self.di` (lines 225-238)
- Computes default `epsilon` as average distance between nodes (lines 245-252)
- Computes weights by solving `linalg.solve(self.A, self.di)` (line 270), where `self.A` is the kernel matrix with smoothing subtracted from diagonal

**Kernel matrix (lines 272-277):** Property `A` computes pairwise distances via `squareform(pdist(self.xi.T, self.norm))` and applies the RBF function.

**Evaluation (lines 282-292):** `__call__` computes distances from evaluation points to data points via `cdist`, applies the RBF, and returns `dot(_function(r), nodes)`.

---

### 2. Modern RBFInterpolator (_rbfinterp.py)

**Class definition (lines 69-220):** Full docstring with mathematical formulation.

**Constructor parameters (lines 74-118):**
- `y`: data point coordinates (npoints, ndims)
- `d`: data values (npoints, ...)
- `neighbors`: nearest neighbors for local interpolation
- `smoothing`: smoothing parameter (default 0.0)
- `kernel`: RBF type ('linear', 'thin_plate_spline', 'cubic', 'quintic', 'multiquadric', 'inverse_multiquadric', 'inverse_quadratic', 'gaussian')
- `epsilon`: shape parameter
- `degree`: polynomial degree (lines 106-118 show minimum degrees per kernel)

**Mathematical formulation (lines 121-151):** The interpolant is `f(x) = K(x, y)a + P(x)b` where coefficients solve:
```
(K(x, y) + λI)a + P(y)b = d
P(y)^T a = 0
```

**Constructor implementation (lines 226-349):**
- Lines 232-233: Select backend (_rbfinterp_np or _rbfinterp_xp)
- Lines 316-335: For `neighbors=None`, call `_build_and_solve_system` to get `shift`, `scale`, `coeffs`
- Lines 338-339: For `neighbors!=None`, build KDTree for nearest neighbor queries

**Evaluation (__call__, lines 449-540):**
- Lines 479-486: Without neighbors, use `_chunk_evaluator` with precomputed coefficients
- Lines 487-536: With neighbors, query KDTree (line 493), build unique neighborhoods (lines 502-504), solve local systems (lines 521-529), and evaluate (lines 530-536)
- Lines 375-447: `_chunk_evaluator` handles memory budgeting by chunking large inputs

**Backend dispatch (lines 51-54):** `_get_backend` returns `_rbfinterp_np` for NumPy arrays, `_rbfinterp_xp` otherwise.

---

### 3. Backend Strategy (_rbfinterp_*.py)

**_rbfinterp_common.py (lines 1-33):** Contains `_monomial_powers_impl`, a pure Python function generating monomial exponent combinations for polynomial terms. Shared by all backends.

**_rbfinterp_np.py (NumPy + Pythran backend):**
- Lines 13-89: `_build_and_solve_system` calls LAPACK's `dsysv` via `scipy.linalg.lapack` for symmetric indefinite system solving
- Lines 17-22: Trampolines to pythran-compiled functions
- Lines 91-95: `compute_interpolation` constructs evaluation coefficients and performs `vec @ coeffs`

**_rbfinterp_pythran.py (Pythran-compiled kernels):**
- Lines 4-48: Kernel function definitions and `NAME_TO_FUNC` mapping
- Lines 79-84, 88-92: Pythran-exported `_kernel_matrix` and `_polynomial_matrix`
- Lines 101-164: `_build_system` constructs the LHS/RHS matrices with domain scaling (shift/scale for numerical stability, lines 136-144)
- Lines 174-215: `_build_evaluation_coefficients` constructs coefficients for evaluation

**_rbfinterp_xp.py (Array API backend, lines 1-27):** Module docstring explains the strategy:
> "The general logic is this: `_rbfinterp.py` implements the user API and calls into either `_rbfinterp_np` (the 'numpy backend'), or `_rbfinterp_xp` (the 'generic backend')."
- Uses vectorized code instead of explicit loops for torch/jax compatibility
- Kernels accept array of norms, not scalar
- Kernels accept `xp` argument for array namespace

**Architecture:** Call chain is `_rbfinterp.py` → `_rbfinterp_np.py` → `_rbfinterp_pythran.py` for NumPy, or `_rbfinterp.py` → `_rbfinterp_xp.py` for other array API compliant libraries (PyTorch, JAX, CuPy).

---

### 4. Rational Interpolation (_bary_rational.py)

**Barycentric form (lines 91-133 in base class):** The evaluation uses the barycentric rational form:
```python
r = CC @ (weights * support_values) / (CC @ weights)
```
where `CC = 1 / subtract.outer(z, support_points)` is a Cauchy matrix.

**AAA class (lines 213-630):** Implements the AAA (Adaptive Antoulas-Anderson) algorithm for rational approximation.
- Lines 214-428: Extensive docstring describing the algorithm
- Lines 458-560: `_compute_weights` implements the greedy AAA iteration:
  - Lines 479-490: Select next support point maximizing residual
  - Lines 496-524: Solve least-squares problem via SVD of Loewner matrix
  - Lines 531-539: Compute rational approximant from weights
- Lines 276-282 (docstring): Barycentric form `r(z) = Σ w_j f_j / (z - z_j) / Σ w_j / (z - z_j)`
- Lines 562-629: `clean_up` method removes Froissart doublets (pole-zero pairs with negligible residue)

**AAA support point selection (lines 476-545):** Greedy algorithm selects point maximizing `|f(z) - n(z)/d(z)|` at each iteration.

**FloaterHormannInterpolator (lines 632-753):** 
- Lines 633-730: Docstring explaining C∞ smooth rational interpolant with no poles on real axis
- Lines 669-684 (docstring): Blends `n-d` polynomials of degree `d` with blending functions `λ_i(x)`
- Lines 690-701 (docstring): Barycentric form with weights computed via alternating sums of reciprocal products
- Lines 743-752: `_compute_weights` implements Floater-Hormann weight computation

**Key difference:** AAA is adaptive (greedy support selection) for complex domains, while Floater-Hormann uses all points with precomputed weights for guaranteed real-axis stability.</result>

## FILE 1: `/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/scipy-scipy/scipy/interpolate/_cubic.py`

### 1. PUBLIC CLASSES AND FUNCTIONS

**CubicHermiteSpline** (lines 85-175)
Piecewise cubic Hermite interpolator fitting values and first derivatives with C1 smoothness. Converts data points and derivatives into PPoly coefficient representation via Hermite basis formulas.

**PchipInterpolator** (lines 184-353)
Shape-preserving PCHIP monotonic cubic interpolator (C1 smooth) using weighted harmonic mean of slopes to prevent overshoot. Implements Fritsch-Butland algorithm for derivative computation at interior and boundary points.

**pchip_interpolate** (lines 356-415)
Convenience function wrapper creating PchipInterpolator and evaluating it, optionally returning derivatives.

**Akima1DInterpolator** (lines 426-637)
"Visually pleasing" Akima interpolator (C1 smooth) using local slope weighting to avoid oscillations; supports both classic Akima and modified Makima methods. Extrapolation defaults to False.

**CubicSpline** (lines 647-1065)
Piecewise cubic interpolator with C2 continuity solving tridiagonal system for derivative values at knots; supports not-a-knot, periodic, natural, and clamped boundary conditions.

### 2. KEY INTERNAL HELPERS AND BASE CLASSES

**prepare_input** (lines 21-74)
Validates and prepares input arrays: converts dtypes, checks finiteness/strictly-increasing property, computes dx differences, and moves interpolation axis to position 0.

**PchipInterpolator._edge_case** (lines 281-294)
One-sided three-point estimate for endpoint derivatives with shape-preserving adjustments (zeroing or clamping when sign changes detected).

**PchipInterpolator._find_derivatives** (lines 296-353)
Core PCHIP algorithm computing derivatives via weighted harmonic mean: w1=2hk+h(k-1), w2=hk+2h(k-1), with zero derivatives when slope signs differ.

**CubicSpline._validate_bc** (lines 996-1064)
Validates and normalizes boundary condition specifications, converting 'natural'/'clamped' strings to (order, value) tuples and checking periodic y[0]==y[-1].

### 3. CUBICSPLINE BOUNDARY CONDITIONS IMPLEMENTATION

**not-a-knot** (lines 957-962, 972-977)
Makes first/second segments share same polynomial at each end. Modifies tridiagonal matrix: A[1,0]=dx[1], A[0,1]=x[2]-x[0] with computed RHS based on slopes.

**periodic** (lines 904-955)
Enforces y'[0]==y'[-1] and y''[0]==y''[-1]. Reduces to (n-1) system, uses "condensed matrix" technique solving two tridiagonal systems (s1, s2) for corner values.

**clamped** (lines 1031-1032, 963-966)
First derivative set to zero at boundaries. Matrix: A[1,0]=1, A[0,1]=0, RHS=b[0]=0. For arbitrary value v: RHS=bc_start[1].

**natural** (lines 1033-1034, 967-970)
Second derivative set to zero at boundaries. Matrix: A[1,0]=2*dx[0], A[0,1]=dx[0], RHS based on finite difference approximation of second derivative.

**Special case n=3 with not-a-knot** (lines 859-877)
Fits parabola through three points using 3x3 dense matrix instead of tridiagonal system.

**Special case n=2** (lines 849-853)
Reduces to linear interpolation with derivatives equal to slope.

### 4. NOTABLE MECHANISMS

**Cubic Hermite Basis** (lines 162-171 in CubicHermiteSpline.__init__)
Coefficients computed as: c = stack([t/dxr, (slope-dydx[:-1])/dxr-t, dydx[:-1], y[:-1]])
where t = (dydx[:-1] + dydx[1:] - 2*slope) / dxr captures curvature.

**Monotone PCHIP** (lines 323-338 in _find_derivatives)
When adjacent slope signs differ or either is zero, derivative set to zero. Otherwise uses weighted harmonic mean: 1/dk = (w1/mk[k-1] + w2/mk[k]) / (w1+w2).

**Akima Weights** (lines 587-615 in Akima1DInterpolator.__init__)
For classic Akima: w1 = |delta[i+1] - delta[i]|, w2 = |delta[i-1] - delta[i-2]|
For Makima: adds |delta[i+1] + delta[i]|/2 term to avoid overshoot and edge cases.
Slope: d[i] = 0.5*(m[i+3] + m[i-3]) initially, then refined via weighted average.

**Tridiagonal Solver** (lines 888-990)
Uses scipy.linalg.solve_banded((1,1), A, b) for efficient O(n) solution of C2 continuity equations.

## FILE 2: `/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/scipy-scipy/scipy/interpolate/_polyint.py`

### 1. PUBLIC CLASSES AND FUNCTIONS

**_Interpolator1D** (lines 22-149)
Base class handling common univariate interpolation: axis manipulation, dtype management (float64/complex128), and y-array reshaping.

**_Interpolator1DWithDerivatives** (lines 151-248)
Mixin base adding derivative evaluation capabilities via _evaluate_derivatives method.

**KroghInterpolator** (lines 251-396)
C∞ polynomial interpolator supporting repeated x values for derivative constraints; uses divided differences algorithm for coefficient computation.

**krogh_interpolate** (lines 398-461)
Convenience function creating KroghInterpolator and evaluating it with optional derivatives.

**approximate_taylor_polynomial** (lines 465-556)
[DEPRECATED] Estimates Taylor polynomial via fitting at clustered points using cosine sampling on interval; returns poly1d.

**BarycentricInterpolator** (lines 559-971)
Numerically stable Lagrange interpolator using barycentric form with efficient y-updates and incremental node addition; supports derivative evaluation via recursive interpolation.

**barycentric_interpolate** (lines 974-1051)
Convenience function creating BarycentricInterpolator and evaluating with optional derivatives.

**lagrange** (not found in these files - likely in separate module)

### 2. KEY INTERNAL HELPERS AND BASE CLASSES

**_isscalar** (lines 17-19)
Utility checking if value is scalar or 0-dimensional array.

**_Interpolator1D._prepare_x** (lines 97-101)
Flattens input x to 1D while preserving original shape for reshaping output.

**_Interpolator1D._finish_y** (lines 103-112)
Reshapes interpolated y back to N-D form with correct axis ordering.

**_Interpolator1D._reshape_yi** (lines 114-120)
Moves interpolation axis to position 0 and flattens trailing dimensions.

**_Interpolator1D._set_dtype** (lines 142-148)
Enforces float64 or complex128 dtype based on input dtype.

**KroghInterpolator coefficient computation** (lines 338-355)
Divided differences algorithm handling repeated x values for derivative conditions. Uses factorial scaling for multiplicities.

### 3. BARYCENTRICINTERPOLATOR: BARYCENTRIC WEIGHTS ALGORITHM

**Initial weight computation** (lines 743-767 in __init__)
If weights wi not provided, computed as:
- Capacity scaling: _inv_capacity = 4.0 / (max(xi) - min(xi))
- Random permutation applied to inputs for numerical stability
- For each i: dist[j] = _inv_capacity * (xi[i] - xi[permuted[j]])
- wi[i] = 1.0 / prod(dist) with dist[i] = 1.0 (self-distance handled)

**Explicit formulas** (documented in lines 620-665)
- Equally spaced: wi = (-1)^i * C(n, i) where C is binomial coefficient
- Chebyshev points: wi = (-1)^i for interior, wi = (-1)^i/2 for endpoints

**Incremental node addition** (lines 832-840 in add_xi)
When adding new points, old weights are inverted, updated via:
- wi[:j] *= _inv_capacity * (xi[new] - xi[:j]) for all existing j
- wi[new] = prod(_inv_capacity * (xi[:new] - xi[new])) for new point
- Then re-invert all weights

### 4. NOTABLE MECHANISMS

**Polynomial interpolation via barycentric form** (lines 620-626, 867-884)
Evaluation uses: p(x) = sum(wi * yi / (x - xi)) / sum(wi / (x - xi))
Avoids explicit polynomial coefficients; handles x == xi cases via zero detection.

**Derivative evaluation** (lines 909-971)
Uses recursive relationship: p'(x) computed via interpolating derivative weights c[i,j] = (wj/wi)/(xi[i]-xi[j]) where diagonal c[j,j] = -sum(c[i,j]) for i != j. Creates nested BarycentricInterpolator for c_ij @ yi.

**Krogh divided differences** (lines 338-355)
Handles repeated x values: when xi[k-s] == xi[k], uses factorial scaling Vk[0] = yi[k]/factorial(s). Builds coefficient c via incremental recurrence.

**Axis handling** (lines 103-112, 114-120)
Sophisticated axis manipulation: moves interpolation axis to position 0 for computation, then restores original axis ordering via transpose with index permutation.

- PPoly class (from scipy.interpolate module, line 14) is the base class for all cubic interpolators
- _isscalar imported from _polyint (line 15) for scalar checking
- Both files use scipy.linalg.solve and solve_banded for tridiagonal systems</result>
